task: Broadcasting to 100k sockets in a single room over Redis adapter saturates pub/sub bandwidth. Implement shard-by-hash to spread load across multiple adapter instances.
reasoning: All emits to a room serialize through one Redis pub/sub channel by default. At 100k sockets × small payload × high frequency, you saturate Redis NIC. Solution: shard rooms — instead of one big room, split into N subrooms by user hash; emit to all shards. Reduces fan-out per channel.
why_this_is_correct: 16 shards = 16 parallel pub/sub channels; load spreads. Targeted emits (to a specific user) hit one shard, not the entire room. Hash-based assignment is deterministic so the same user always joins the same shard. Cost: full broadcast is 16 emits instead of 1, but each touches 1/16th the sockets.
what_breaks_naive_approach: One mega-room: every emit fan-outs through one Redis channel; bandwidth pegs, all rooms suffer. Per-user direct emit: scales worse (100k sends per broadcast). Manual sharding without hash function: re-balancing on user join causes re-subscription storms.

task: Client emits while offline are lost. Buffer them locally and flush in order on reconnect, deduplicating by client-side id.
reasoning: socket.io-client has a built-in send buffer but only for emits made WHILE connected (after the connection is lost). Calls made when never-connected go through, but the order isn't guaranteed across restart. Wrap with an explicit outbox: store pending emits in memory (or IndexedDB), flush in order on connect, drop on ack.
why_this_is_correct: Sequential flush (one-at-a-time await) preserves order. __outboxId travels with payload so server deduplicates (combines with idempotency middleware). Failed acks halt the queue so we don't reorder. inFlight set prevents double-send if connect fires while flushing.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Relying on socket.io's volatile send buffer: order isn't preserved across full reconnections, and emits before initial connect race with the connect event. Parallel flush sends out-of-order writes. No dedup id = duplicates after retry storms.

task: Track user presence (online/offline) across a multi-instance Socket.IO cluster using Redis with TTL.
reasoning: Per-instance Map<userId,bool> doesn't survive restart and doesn't see other pods. Use Redis SET key with TTL extended on heartbeat. Presence = key exists. When TTL expires (no heartbeat for N seconds), user counts as offline.
why_this_is_correct: TTL = absolute upper bound on staleness if a pod crashes without firing disconnect. Heartbeat extends TTL while connection lives. markOffline compares socketId — if user has another active socket on a different pod, that pod's heartbeat keeps the key alive even after this socket drops.
what_breaks_naive_approach: In-memory Map loses presence on restart. No TTL means crashed pods leave users 'online' forever. Deleting on disconnect without comparing socketId kicks the user offline even when they have another tab/device connected.

task: Replace Redis adapter with the cluster adapter for single-host Node cluster mode without external dependencies.
reasoning: For single-host scaling (Node cluster module), Redis is overkill. @socket.io/cluster-adapter uses IPC between cluster workers. Each worker still has its own sockets, and emits to rooms are forwarded to siblings via cluster IPC.
why_this_is_correct: Sticky session at the primary routes a connection's polling and websocket to the same worker. Cluster adapter forwards emits between workers via Node IPC — no Redis. Respawn on exit keeps the cluster size stable.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Vanilla cluster mode: polling handshakes round-robin to different workers, ~50% fail with 'Session ID unknown'. Skipping cluster adapter: emits to rooms only reach the same-worker sockets. Using Redis here adds a network hop you don't need.

task: Dynamic namespace routing: every /chat/:roomId becomes a separate namespace so room-level lifecycle hooks are isolated.
reasoning: Socket.IO supports regex namespaces: io.of(/^\/chat\/[a-z0-9-]+$/). Each unique URL becomes its own namespace with its own middleware and adapter state. Good fit when rooms have very different lifecycles.
why_this_is_correct: Regex namespace pattern enforces URL shape AND scopes per-room. Per-namespace middleware runs once per connection attempt — auth applies to every chat room transparently. onCreate/onClose hooks fire exactly once per room lifecycle.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single namespace + room as event field: noisy emits broadcast across rooms before client-side filtering. Per-room manual namespace registration: doesn't scale to user-created rooms. Forgetting fetchSockets check fires onClose every disconnect, not just the last.

task: Multiple React components each create their own socket — server sees N connections per user. Centralize as a module-level singleton with lazy init.
reasoning: Component-level socket creation in useEffect = one socket per mount per component. With several listening components on the same page = several connections. Lazy module-level singleton: created on first access, shared across components, disposed on app unmount (rare).
why_this_is_correct: Singleton ensures one socket per browser tab — server connection count matches user count. autoConnect:false + explicit connect prevents the side-effect on import (test friendliness). disposeSocket exposed for tests/HMR.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Each component running `io(url)` in useEffect = N sockets per user. Server billing per-connection becomes expensive; presence tracking gets weird. Shutting down one component disconnects the shared connection if you use it without ref counting.

task: Implement OAuth Authorization Code + PKCE flow for a SPA — generate code_verifier, exchange, validate state.
reasoning: PKCE prevents code interception by binding the auth request to the token exchange via a hash. State parameter prevents CSRF. Verifier must be high-entropy random, stored client-side temporarily (sessionStorage so it doesn't leak across tabs).
why_this_is_correct: base64url encoding (not base64) avoids URL-unsafe chars. 32 bytes (256 bits) of entropy meets RFC 7636. S256 method is required for public clients. State validation on callback (compare to stored state) blocks CSRF; verifier validation at exchange blocks intercepted-code reuse.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Skipping PKCE on public clients: code can be intercepted (e.g., via mobile app deep link) and exchanged by attacker. Skipping state: malicious redirect_uri params attach a victim to attacker's account. Storing verifier in localStorage: leaks across tabs and XSS-reachable.

task: Defend against session fixation: rotate the session id on every privilege change (login, logout, role escalation).
reasoning: Attacker plants a known session id on the victim, victim logs in, attacker uses the same id. Fix: regenerate session id on login. express-session has req.session.regenerate. Copy needed fields before regen because regen wipes the session.
why_this_is_correct: regenerate() issues a fresh sid; the attacker's known id is now stale. Preserve only fields that must survive (CSRF token to avoid form re-fetch). destroy() at logout fully kills the session, clearCookie removes the client copy.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Setting req.session.userId without regen: attacker who planted the sid pre-login now has access. Not preserving CSRF token: in-flight forms fail after login. Forgetting clearCookie: browser keeps the dead sid and presents it forever (annoying log noise).

task: Mass assignment vulnerability: PATCH /me accepts the whole body and lets users set `role: 'admin'`. Build a per-route field whitelist.
reasoning: Spreading req.body into model updates is a classic vector. Whitelist allowed fields at the boundary using Zod's pick/omit, or a typed allowlist function. Don't blacklist — new fields auto-leak.
why_this_is_correct: .strict() on Zod schema rejects unknown keys at parse time — `role:'admin'` triggers 422. Even if validation were laxer, the schema doesn't include role so it would be stripped. Selecting -passwordHash on return prevents leaking sensitive fields.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `UserModel.findByIdAndUpdate(userId, req.body)`: anyone can promote themselves to admin or change `passwordHash`. Blacklisting role only protects against today's threat — adding `permissions` field tomorrow re-opens the hole.

task: Rotate JWT signing keys without invalidating existing tokens. Implement JWKS-style key-id (kid) header for graceful rotation.
reasoning: JWTs include a `kid` header pointing to the key used to sign. Server keeps a key map; rotation = add a new key, sign new tokens with it, keep old keys for verification until their tokens expire (max age). Standard practice for OIDC providers.
why_this_is_correct: Active kid is the signing key; verifier picks the matching key from the header. Adding a new key + setting active rolls forward; retiring old keys after max token age cleans up. Algorithm pinning prevents algorithm confusion attacks.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single secret hot-swap invalidates all existing tokens — every user gets logged out. Rotating with no kid header means verification has to try every key, slow and ambiguous. Sharing key across services blocks per-service rotation.

task: Admin routes should only be reachable from corp VPN IPs. Build a CIDR-aware allowlist middleware that respects X-Forwarded-For when trust proxy is set.
reasoning: Comparing req.ip to a literal IP fails for CIDR ranges (192.168.0.0/16). Need a parser for CIDR + a check against req.ip (which already respects trust proxy in Express when configured). Multiple ranges via array.
why_this_is_correct: BigInt arithmetic handles both IPv4 and IPv6 uniformly. Mask is precomputed once at startup. Family check prevents matching IPv4 mapped into IPv6 form against an IPv4 range. Uses req.ip which honors trust proxy.
what_breaks_naive_approach: String comparison against a literal IP misses ranges. Trusting raw X-Forwarded-For without trust proxy lets attackers spoof their IP. Forgetting IPv6 = breaks for any client on IPv6 corp network.

task: Add JSON body size limit and url-encoded limit to Express to prevent memory exhaustion from large payloads.
reasoning: express.json() defaults to 100kb but apps often raise it carelessly. Set appropriate per-route limits and reject early. Also limit url-encoded for forms.
why_this_is_correct: Strict JSON mode rejects non-object/array roots (subtle attack surface). parameterLimit on urlencoded caps the number of fields. 413 handler returns clean JSON instead of letting the body-parser HTML page bubble up.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Default 100kb might be too permissive for one endpoint while too tight for another (file metadata uploads). Skipping the error-type handler returns ugly HTML. parameterLimit=1000 (default) lets attackers send 1000-field forms that crush form processing.

task: Frontend can GET but POST with custom header gets blocked by CORS preflight. Configure allowedHeaders and methods correctly.
reasoning: Browsers preflight (OPTIONS) for non-simple requests. The server must echo back the requested headers in Access-Control-Allow-Headers. Forgetting a custom header (X-CSRF-Token, X-Request-Id) blocks the actual request silently.
why_this_is_correct: allowedHeaders must include every custom header the frontend sends. exposedHeaders is what the frontend can READ from responses (default is a tiny safe-list). maxAge caches preflight 10min so subsequent requests skip OPTIONS. Origin callback enables dynamic allowlists.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Defaults block X-CSRF-Token; frontend silently fails with no useful console message. Setting origin: true with credentials reflects any origin = total bypass. Not handling OPTIONS explicitly relies on cors middleware ordering — break it and you lose preflight handling.

task: Context-based store tears under React 19 concurrent rendering when multiple components read it during a transition. Fix with useSyncExternalStore.
reasoning: During concurrent rendering, React may render with stale data while another tree finishes. Standard useState/useContext doesn't guarantee tearing-free reads. useSyncExternalStore is designed for this: tells React to suspend or restart a render if the snapshot changes mid-render.
why_this_is_correct: useSyncExternalStore is the React-blessed way to subscribe to external mutable state without tearing under concurrent rendering. getServerSnapshot (the third arg) matches the client snapshot — safe for SSR. Ref-stored store has stable identity, doesn't change between renders.
what_breaks_naive_approach: useState in provider re-renders all consumers per update. Plain ref + forceUpdate hooks tear during concurrent transitions. Class-based store with eventEmitter and manual setState bypasses React's concurrent guarantees.

task: Reducer is called twice for each action in StrictMode dev. Refactor so the reducer is pure and idempotent.
reasoning: React 19 StrictMode invokes setState callbacks and reducers twice in dev to surface side effects. The reducer MUST be pure — no I/O, no Date.now, no random. Side effects go in handlers (event callbacks, useEffect), not in the reducer.
why_this_is_correct: Side effects (Date.now) live in handlers, not the reducer. Reducer takes the timestamp as part of the action — calling it twice gives the same result. Double-invocation in StrictMode is now safe and proves purity.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Calling Date.now() inside the reducer: StrictMode's second invocation overwrites the first with a newer timestamp — tests are flaky. Pushing to a global array in the reducer doubles entries in dev.

task: Wrap a fetch in a React 19 Suspense-compatible resource that throws a promise on first read and resolves on success.
reasoning: Suspense for data: a component that reads not-yet-resolved data should throw the promise so Suspense can catch it. Cache by key so concurrent components reading the same resource share one fetch. Important: don't create the resource inside render — create externally or with React's use().
why_this_is_correct: Throwing the promise integrates with Suspense — boundary shows fallback until promise resolves. Caching by key prevents N components fetching the same data N times. Manual invalidate for mutations. React 19's use() hook does similar but knowing the underlying pattern is essential.
what_breaks_naive_approach: useState + useEffect for data: pre-render flash of empty state, race with Suspense. Creating resource inside render: re-creates each render, infinite fetch loop. No cache: every component re-fetches.

task: Need element height measured AFTER it renders, but useRef + useEffect runs after paint and causes a flash. Use ref callback for synchronous measurement.
reasoning: useRef captures the DOM node, but the effect runs in commit phase — after the browser may have painted. For layout calculations that affect render, use a ref callback: it runs synchronously when the node mounts/unmounts, before paint.
why_this_is_correct: Ref callback runs synchronously at attach. Initial measurement happens before next paint, ResizeObserver picks up subsequent changes. React 19's cleanup return on ref callbacks lets you unhook the observer.
what_breaks_naive_approach: useRef + useEffect measures after paint, causing a one-frame flash where the element renders at default size before layout adjusts. Polling via setTimeout is wasteful and laggy.

task: Counter component with rapid double-click increments only by 1 instead of 2. Show the functional updater fix.
reasoning: setState(count + 1) closes over the stale count from the render that registered the handler. Two clicks in the same tick both see count=0, both setCount(1). Use the functional form setState(c => c + 1) to receive the latest queued state.
why_this_is_correct: Functional updater receives the most-recently-queued state, so two batched updates correctly produce +2. Same pattern needed inside async callbacks where the closure captures old state.
what_breaks_naive_approach: setCount(count + 1); setCount(count + 1): both reads see count=0, both queue setCount(1) — final state is 1, not 2. Common surprise for new React devs.

task: Component subscribes to a window event but cleanup doesn't fire because the handler is recreated each render. Fix with useCallback or stable handler ref.
reasoning: addEventListener+removeEventListener require the SAME function reference. Inline arrow functions are new references each render. Either define the handler outside the component, useCallback with stable deps, or define inside the effect (registered + removed with same closure).
why_this_is_correct: Handler defined inside the effect = same reference for add and remove. Empty deps array means the effect runs once on mount, registers, returns cleanup. handler() initial call captures the size in case window resized between render and effect commit.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Defining handler in component body and passing to addEventListener: each render adds a new listener; cleanup removes a different one — listeners pile up. Inline arrow: same problem but worse because there's no chance to remove the right reference.

task: Loaded GLTF model is too big and offset; correctly position, scale, and center it programmatically.
reasoning: GLTF models come with arbitrary origin and scale. Compute bounding box, translate so center is at origin (or feet on ground plane), scale to a target size. Do this once after load, not per frame.
why_this_is_correct: Bounding box gives actual rendered extents. Scale to target size based on longest dimension preserves proportions. Optional groundY puts feet on a plane (common for character models); otherwise center vertically. Recomputing the box after scale ensures center is correct.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hard-coded scale 0.01: works for one model, breaks for the next. Computing center before scaling and using it post-scale: off by the scale factor. Forgetting to recompute box after scale = wrong center.

task: Add a custom dissolve effect (noise-based fade) to a Three.js StandardMaterial without writing a full custom shader. Use onBeforeCompile.
reasoning: Writing a ShaderMaterial means re-implementing all of MeshStandardMaterial's lighting. onBeforeCompile lets you patch the built-in shader strings before compilation. Add a uniform + GLSL noise function + clip pixels below threshold.
why_this_is_correct: onBeforeCompile preserves all of StandardMaterial's PBR lighting; we only inject noise + clip. Returning uniforms object lets the caller animate uThreshold each frame. discard kills pixels below threshold for transparent dissolve. Edge band glows for that classic 'burn' look.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Writing a ShaderMaterial from scratch: no lighting/shadows unless you reimplement them. Modifying material.userData has no effect on shaders. Forgetting to pass uniforms.value reference means animations don't propagate.

task: Scene with 1000 distant objects drops FPS even when most are off-screen. Diagnose and enable proper frustum culling + LOD.
reasoning: three.js culls each mesh against the camera frustum by default. Bug: objects inside large parent groups inherit the parent's bounding box — if it's huge, nothing culls. Fix: ensure each Mesh has computed bounding sphere/box; for instanced meshes, set bounding sphere manually. For very-distant objects, use LOD with a simple imposter at long distance.
why_this_is_correct: Bounding sphere computation enables per-mesh frustum tests. LOD swaps to cheaper geometry beyond distance thresholds. For very large scenes, this is more effective than postprocessing tweaks.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Nesting meshes inside a Group with frustumCulled=false (sometimes set by import scripts) skips culling for the whole subtree. Missing boundingSphere means culling test trivially passes — every mesh draws regardless of camera.

task: Static three.js scene runs at 60fps even when idle, wasting battery. Switch to on-demand rendering with invalidate().
reasoning: R3F has frameloop='demand' that only renders when invalidate() is called or props change. Perfect for product viewers, hero sections — anything not continuously animated. Wire up controls to invalidate; otherwise the scene appears frozen.
why_this_is_correct: frameloop='demand' skips render when nothing changed. Invalidating on controls 'change' event renders during user interaction. R3F's makeDefault on controls makes them discoverable via useThree.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Default frameloop='always': 60fps battery drain even on a static gallery page. Switching to demand without wiring controls = camera moves but nothing redraws (looks broken).

task: Throwing a synchronous error in middleware crashes the process because the error wasn't caught by Express error middleware. Wrap routers safely.
reasoning: Express 5 catches async rejections; Express 4 doesn't. Even in 5, synchronous throws from inside event handlers (like a setImmediate or socket callback) escape Express. Wrap all entry points with try/catch → next(err) or use a wrapper.
why_this_is_correct: safeHandler handles both sync throws and promise rejections in one wrapper. safeRouter wraps every route registration so you can't forget. Express 5's built-in async catching is good but explicit is safer for portability.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Unwrapped sync throw in a non-async handler bypasses Express: bubbles to Node, crashes process. Async-only catchers miss sync code in mixed handlers. Per-route try/catch is boilerplate everyone forgets.

task: Worker job sometimes hangs forever. Add per-job timeout + cancellation token so the worker can move on.
reasoning: setTimeout that 'aborts' is only effective if the job code respects an AbortSignal. Real cancellation requires the job to check signal.aborted at safe points or pass the signal into I/O calls. Promise.race wins us the timeout but the orphaned promise keeps running unless given a signal.
why_this_is_correct: Job receives the AbortSignal so cooperative cancellation is possible (pass to fetch, DB drivers that support it). Promise.race ensures the caller sees the timeout immediately, even if the underlying work continues. clearTimeout in finally prevents the timer firing after success.
what_breaks_naive_approach: setTimeout(reject, n) without AbortSignal: caller moves on but the work keeps running, holding resources. No signal in JobContext means jobs can't cooperate. Forgetting clearTimeout = harmless but logs ugly aborts after completion.

task: Errors logged without request context are useless. Attach request id, user id, and route to every error report.
reasoning: Centralized error middleware sees the error and the request. Enrich with AsyncLocalStorage context + req metadata before shipping to logger/Sentry. Strip headers like Authorization before logging.
why_this_is_correct: Header redaction prevents leaking auth tokens to logs/Sentry. requestId in the response lets users quote it in support tickets. headersSent check avoids the double-send crash if the error fires mid-response.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Logging raw headers leaks Bearer tokens to log aggregator. No requestId means correlating user-reported issues to log lines is impossible. Sending response after headersSent throws a second error and confuses the trace.

task: Demonstrate Liskov substitution with a Notifier hierarchy: EmailNotifier and SmsNotifier substitute for the base Notifier without surprising callers.
reasoning: LSP: subclasses must satisfy the same contract as the parent — same input range, same output shape, no stricter preconditions, no looser postconditions. Common violation: subclass throws on inputs the base accepts.
why_this_is_correct: canDeliver lets callers check applicability without subclasses throwing on mismatched input. Both subclasses return NotificationResult — no broken postcondition. dispatch treats all Notifiers identically.
what_breaks_naive_approach: SmsNotifier.send throws on non-phone recipient: caller must know about subclass-specific exceptions, breaking polymorphism. Different return shapes per subclass (some return boolean, some return string) make composition painful.

task: Model a payment with three variants (card, bank, paypal) using Mongoose discriminators for clean TS narrowing.
reasoning: Discriminators store all variants in one collection with a discriminator key. Mongoose discriminators integrate with TS unions. Each variant has its own schema + class; the base class holds shared fields.
why_this_is_correct: Discriminator key persisted alongside data lets us narrow Payment to the right variant in TS. Exhaustive switch ensures adding a new kind triggers compile errors in describePayment. Single collection = efficient queries across all kinds.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Separate collections per kind: cross-cutting queries (all payments for user) need N collection reads. Boolean flags on a flat schema with optional fields per kind: TS can't narrow, runtime checks scattered everywhere.

task: Filtering a large list as user types blocks input. Wrap the filter state update in startTransition so input stays responsive.
reasoning: React 19's useTransition marks a state update as non-urgent. React keeps input responsive (urgent update) and renders the filtered list in the background; if user types again, the in-progress render is discarded.
why_this_is_correct: Input value (query) updates urgently — stays in sync with keystrokes. Filter input (deferredQuery) is a transition, batched and interruptible. isPending flag shows a hint without blocking input. Cap list at 200 to keep DOM bounded.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Direct setState on each keystroke: 10k-item filter blocks for 30ms, input lag visible. Debouncing helps but adds delay before results appear; transitions feel instant for fast users but yield the main thread.

task: CPU-bound image hashing blocks Express requests. Offload to worker_threads with a pool and typed messages.
reasoning: Node is single-threaded for JS. CPU work blocks all requests. worker_threads runs JS in parallel threads. Pool pattern: pre-spawn N workers, queue tasks, return results via Promise.
why_this_is_correct: Fixed pool prevents unbounded thread spawning. Queue ensures fair scheduling. Worker errors return to caller via reject. terminate() cleans up on shutdown. Workers run independent V8 isolates — true parallelism for CPU work.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Inline crypto.createHash for 50MB images blocks the event loop for hundreds of ms — all other requests wait. Spawning a new Worker per request leaks threads. Not catching worker errors leaves promises hanging forever.

task: Fan out Mongoose change events to Socket.IO clients via change streams without losing events during reconnect.
reasoning: MongoDB change streams give a resumable cursor. Save the resume token so reconnects pick up where left off (no data loss). Stream change docs into Socket.IO emits, filtered by user/room scope.
why_this_is_correct: resumeAfter with saved token = no missed events across restart/reconnect (within oplog retention). fullDocument:'updateLookup' fetches the full doc on update (otherwise change has only the diff). Per-user room scoping avoids broadcasting cross-tenant data.
what_breaks_naive_approach: No resume token: every reconnect loses any events during downtime. fullDocument default omits non-updated fields — frontend must re-fetch or work with deltas. Broadcasting all changes to all clients = privacy leak + bandwidth.

task: Implement a reliable task queue with Redis Streams (XADD/XREADGROUP) and consumer ack — survives worker crashes mid-task.
reasoning: Streams + consumer groups give pending-entry tracking so unacked tasks are reassigned when a worker dies. Pattern: XADD to enqueue, XREADGROUP to read as a consumer, XACK on success, XAUTOCLAIM to take over abandoned messages.
why_this_is_correct: XAUTOCLAIM reclaims messages idle > N ms — recovers from crashed consumers. XACK only on success means failed work re-runs (at-least-once). MKSTREAM creates stream if absent. BUSYGROUP error on group create = group already exists = ignore. BLOCK 5000 makes XREADGROUP wait instead of busy-loop.
what_breaks_naive_approach: List-based queue (LPUSH/RPOP) loses messages on crash: a worker that LPOPped but crashed before processing drops the task. No autoclaim = messages stuck in PEL forever. Acking before processing converts to at-most-once = silent data loss.

task: Implement an exhaustive switch helper using `never` so adding a new union member breaks compilation in every dispatch site.
reasoning: Switch over a discriminated union should be checked at compile time for completeness. Trick: in the default branch, assign the value to a const of type never. If you add a new union member, default reaches non-never value and fails.
why_this_is_correct: If you add `{ kind: 'pentagon'; sides: number[] }` to Shape, the default branch's shape is no longer narrowed to never — TS compile error pinpoints every dispatch site. Runtime fallback gives a useful error for unexpected data (from JSON, etc.).
what_breaks_naive_approach: No default + no exhaustive narrowing: missed case returns undefined silently. Default that returns 0 hides the missing case at compile and runtime. Throw 'unknown' without never type: works at runtime but offers no compile-time safety.

task: Build a typed query builder where chained where/orderBy/select methods narrow the return type so unselected fields can't be accessed.
reasoning: Method chaining + generics let the builder track which fields are selected. Use phantom types for selected keys; the final exec returns Pick<T, Selected>. Without runtime overhead beyond what the methods do anyway.
why_this_is_correct: Selected keys flow through generics so exec returns the precise Pick. Each call returns a new builder so chaining doesn't mutate state. The runtime trims fields client-side after fetch (or you can pass select to your DB layer for true projection).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Untyped builders return T[] always — callers see all fields even when select narrowed them. Mutating state methods break method-chain immutability assumptions. Type assertions in exec lose the precision the generics tracked.

task: Single source of truth pattern: derive TypeScript type from a Zod schema and ensure they never drift.
reasoning: Maintaining a TS interface AND a Zod schema for the same shape invites drift. Define schema once, derive type. Bonus: a function that converts validated input into the typed shape with no extra cost.
why_this_is_correct: z.infer derives the output type (after transforms). z.input is the pre-transform shape — useful for API request DTOs where the client sends one shape and the server parses into another. coerce.date handles ISO strings from JSON.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Maintaining `interface User` AND `UserSchema` side by side: adding a field to one without the other compiles, fails at runtime in surprising places. Casting `as User` after fetch loses validation. Hand-written validators drift from the type.

task: Enable @socket.io/admin-ui safely in production with auth so ops can inspect live connections.
reasoning: admin-ui surfaces live socket counts, rooms, and event sampling. Out-of-the-box it's unauthenticated. Configure with credentials + restricted CORS origin.
why_this_is_correct: Argon2 hashed password (admin-ui requires bcrypt-compatible — bcryptjs also works) prevents plaintext leakage. mode:'production' enables minimal sampling to reduce overhead. Restricting CORS to admin origin prevents cross-origin admin access.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Default no-auth = anyone with admin-ui URL can browse your sockets. Plaintext password in config = on-disk leak. Wildcard CORS = admin UI accessible from any origin.

task: Append-only audit log with hash chain so tampering shows up as a broken chain.
reasoning: Each entry includes the hash of the previous entry — modifying any past entry breaks subsequent hashes. Store in DB with prev_hash column. Verification recomputes the chain.
why_this_is_correct: Each hash binds to previous via prevHash — tampering with entry N breaks N+1, N+2, ... Append serializes via unique seq constraint (Mongo enforces). verify rebuilds the chain and reports the first broken seq. Pair with periodic external hash publication for stronger guarantees.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Plain insert-only logs can be edited by anyone with DB access without detection. Storing only timestamps doesn't prevent reordering. Skipping seq uniqueness allows duplicate appends to confuse verification.

task: Component receives userId prop; internal draft state should reset when userId changes but persist across other re-renders. Use the key prop trick.
reasoning: Manually resetting state in useEffect [userId] adds an extra render and easy bugs. The React-idiomatic fix: key the component by userId from the parent — React unmounts and remounts when key changes, fresh state automatically.
why_this_is_correct: key={userId} forces React to treat the component as a different instance when userId changes — full unmount/mount cycle. All internal state, refs, effects reset cleanly. Zero useEffect plumbing.
what_breaks_naive_approach: useEffect([userId]) to reset draft: extra render with stale value briefly visible. setDraft inside render breaks React's rules. Forgetting to reset altogether leaves the previous user's edits showing for the new user.

task: Centralized error middleware logs 4xx errors at error level, flooding alerting. Distinguish client errors (warn) from server errors (error).
reasoning: 4xx = user fault, expected, don't page someone. 5xx = our problem, page someone. Log at the right level so alert rules can trigger only on real fires. Operational errors carry statusCode — use it.
why_this_is_correct: 4xx routed to warn (countable in dashboards but not paging). 5xx and unknown errors go to error level with full stack. Alert rules can fire on log level + message — keeps pages real.
what_breaks_naive_approach: All errors logged at error level: 404s from buggy clients page the on-call at 3am. Logging only 500s misses early-warning signs (sudden spike in 401s). Returning err.message without stripping for 5xx leaks internals.

task: Heavy read endpoint denormalizes 5 collections per call. Introduce a read model populated from domain events, separate from the write model.
reasoning: CQRS-light: write model is normalized (3NF), read model is a denormalized projection optimized for queries. Domain events from writes update the projection. Reads hit a single collection. Eventual consistency tradeoff.
why_this_is_correct: Reads hit one indexed collection — sub-ms. Write side stays normalized so authoritative data isn't duplicated. Domain events update projection idempotently (use _id = orderId to avoid duplicates on event replay). Trade: eventual consistency between write and read — display a spinner briefly if needed.
what_breaks_naive_approach: 5-join aggregation on every read: 100ms+ p99, scales badly. Caching the joined result invalidates on every write to any source — brittle. CQRS adds complexity but pays off when reads vastly outnumber writes.

task: Expensive GET endpoint reads from DB every time. Implement stale-while-revalidate caching with Redis.
reasoning: SWR: serve cached value immediately, refresh in background if past freshness threshold. Two TTLs: fresh (return cached, no refresh), stale (return cached, kick off refresh). Refresh uses an in-flight lock to prevent stampedes.
why_this_is_correct: Fresh: zero latency, no fetch. Stale: zero perceived latency, async refresh. Cold/expired: blocking fetch. inflight Map deduplicates refresh requests so 100 concurrent stale hits trigger one fetch. Per-request timeout on refresh prevents stuck inflight entries.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Plain TTL cache: at expiry, every concurrent request races to fetch — thundering herd. No inflight dedup = same. Synchronous refresh on every request defeats the cache.

task: Type-state pattern: a Builder where calling required methods is enforced at compile time before build() is allowed.
reasoning: Track which methods have been called as a phantom type parameter. build() requires the full set of required keys to have been set. Optional methods don't affect the type-state.
why_this_is_correct: Method return type accumulates the called methods into S. build is a `this` method requiring S to be a superset of required keys — TS rejects builder instances that haven't reached that state. Runtime stores values normally; types do the policing.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Throwing in build() at runtime if a field is missing: production crash instead of compile error. Marking all builder fields as private and requiring them in constructor sacrifices the fluent ergonomics.

task: Switch from Redis adapter to MongoDB adapter for teams already running Mongo at scale — no extra infra.
reasoning: @socket.io/mongo-adapter uses a capped collection as a pub/sub channel. Trade-off: higher latency than Redis (~10ms) but no extra dependency. Setup: create capped collection sized for peak event volume, then attach.
why_this_is_correct: Capped collection auto-prunes oldest events — fixed memory. Size 100MB / 100k entries covers most workloads with retention for late-joining adapters. addCreatedAtField helps with diagnostics. WebSocket-only transport avoids the polling-handshake stickiness problem.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Non-capped collection grows forever, taking down Mongo. No size cap means a traffic spike fills disk. Using polling transport with mongo adapter still needs sticky sessions on the LB.

task: Implement sliding session refresh: access token expires in 15min, but if user is active within 10min, issue a new access token transparently.
reasoning: Sliding sessions balance security (short token life) with UX (no random logouts). On each authenticated request, if access token is past 'soft expiry' threshold, attach a new one via response header. Client picks it up.
why_this_is_correct: Active users get token refreshed every ~5min within a 15min window — they never see logout. Inactive users' tokens expire normally. Client checks X-New-Access-Token header and stores it. No refresh-token round trip on active paths.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Long-lived access tokens: stolen token usable for hours. Refresh on every request: ledger of token issuance balloons. Fixed expiry without sliding: active users get logged out mid-session.

task: Generic typed form hook without external libs: track values, errors, touched state per field with type-safe field paths.
reasoning: For simple forms, react-hook-form is overkill. Build a tiny typed alternative: state holds values/errors/touched maps keyed by field name. Validation is a function. Field paths are typed via keyof T.
why_this_is_correct: Generic T flows into all field operations — typos are caught at compile. fieldProps spreads value/onChange/onBlur with the correct types. validate runs on submit, errors marked touched so they show. submitting flag disables UI during submission.
what_breaks_naive_approach: useState per field gets unwieldy past 5 fields. Untyped values: `values.emial` typo compiles. Manual touched/error tracking duplicates state across fields.

task: Show correct middleware order in Socket.IO: auth → rate-limit → audit, with one middleware short-circuiting the rest cleanly.
reasoning: io.use registers middleware that runs sequentially on connection. Each can call next() to continue or next(error) to reject. Order matters: cheap checks first, expensive last. Errors propagate to client via connect_error.
why_this_is_correct: Auth first — no point rate-limiting an unauth'd token. Rate-limit before audit — failed rate-limit doesn't pollute audit log with garbage. Audit runs once and never errors. Errors via next(err) become 'connect_error' on the client.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Rate-limit before auth: attacker without credentials still consumes rate-limit slots. Audit before rate-limit: fills logs during a flood. Forgetting to call next() hangs the connection forever.

task: Webhook receiver must prevent replay attacks. Combine timestamp window with a nonce cache.
reasoning: Timestamp alone allows replay within the tolerance window. Add a one-time nonce stored in Redis with TTL = tolerance window. First receipt records nonce; replays within window collide.
why_this_is_correct: Tolerance window bounds nonce storage cost — Redis only stores nonces for 5 minutes. NX SET claims the nonce atomically. Signature covers ts + nonce + body so attackers can't swap nonces. timingSafeEqual prevents leaking signature bytes.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Timestamp without nonce: replay within 5min works. Nonce without timestamp: unbounded nonce storage. Non-atomic 'check then set' for nonce: two replays race past the check.

task: Demonstrate `satisfies` vs type annotation: capture wide literal types while still enforcing a constraint.
reasoning: `const x: T = ...` widens types to T, losing literal precision. `x as T` opt out of checking. `x satisfies T` checks against T but preserves the literal narrow type. Crucial for config maps where you want both narrow lookup and shape validation.
why_this_is_correct: satisfies + as const = narrow literal types preserved (keyof gives exact path strings) AND the shape is checked against Route. Lookup typing is precise: methodFor('/admin/users/delete') returns 'DELETE', not the wider union.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Plain annotation `const routes: Record<string, Route>`: keyof is string, lookup loses precision. `as` cast: skips type checking entirely. Without as const, method values widen to string.

task: Refactor a long if/else chain in a webhook router into a Strategy registry keyed by event type, with TypeScript exhaustiveness.
reasoning: Webhook router with `if (type === 'invoice.paid') ... else if (type === 'subscription.created') ...` becomes unmanageable. Map of handlers keyed by event type, each handler typed for its specific payload.
why_this_is_correct: Mapped type ensures the handler's data parameter matches the event's declared payload. Adding a new event = add to map + register handler. Unhandled events return {handled:false} — caller decides what to do (log, ignore, alert).
what_breaks_naive_approach: if/else chain: adding events touches one big function, no per-event type narrowing. Untyped { [type: string]: (data: any) => void }: handlers can't trust payload shape. switch with assertNever-like check works but is more verbose.

task: Page below-the-fold images all download immediately, blowing the perf budget. Switch to native lazy loading.
reasoning: is the modern, zero-dep way to defer offscreen images. width/height attributes prevent layout shift. Add proper srcset for responsive images.
why_this_is_correct: loading='lazy' = browser defers loading until near viewport. decoding='async' = browser decodes off-main-thread. width/height in HTML reserve space, eliminating CLS. srcset picks appropriate resolution per screen.
what_breaks_naive_approach: No loading attr: all images download on page load. Missing width/height: layout shifts as images load (Core Web Vitals penalty). JS-based intersection-observer lazy loading is now overkill — native attribute suffices.

task: User reconnects to a different server in the cluster — they're no longer in their game room. Pin sticky room membership in Redis so any server can restore it.
reasoning: socket.join is per-connection on one server. On reconnect to another server, the new socket has no rooms. Solution: keep an external membership store (userId → roomIds[]); on connection, re-join from the store. Update store on join/leave.
why_this_is_correct: Redis SET tracks intended membership independent of which server the connection landed on. On reconnect to any node, restoreOnConnect re-joins the rooms locally. Explicit clearUser for logout — otherwise old rooms persist until user re-joins.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Relying on Socket.IO's adapter to restore rooms: it doesn't — adapter routes emits, not memberships. In-memory map: lost on restart, doesn't span servers. Without restore, users silently miss room events after reconnect.

task: Stream a large server-computed response back via repeated socket events with progress, not one giant emit.
reasoning: Large single emit blocks the engine and may exceed buffer limits. Chunk on the server, emit progress events with sequence numbers, client assembles. Combines well with backpressure handling.
why_this_is_correct: Session id correlates chunks to this hook instance — multiple components issuing the same query don't mix. Cleanup cancels the server work. Progress derives from seq/totalSeq for stable progress bar. data is rebuilt as a fresh array per chunk to satisfy React's referential equality.
what_breaks_naive_approach: One huge emit blocks the engine for seconds. Without session id, two queries' chunks interleave in state. No cancel sends server racing through cancelled work after user navigated away.

task: Password reset via email link. Generate a secure one-time token, store hash (not plaintext), expire on use.
reasoning: Reset tokens are credentials — leak from DB dump = account takeover. Store hash, like passwords. Set short TTL. Mark used after success. Compare timing-safely.
why_this_is_correct: argon2.verify is timing-safe by construction. expireAfterSeconds:0 index lets Mongo auto-delete expired tokens. usedAt marker prevents double-use. Composite link (resetId + rawToken) avoids guessable monotonic ids — attacker needs both pieces.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Storing plaintext token: DB dump = account takeovers. Long-lived token: stolen email link works for days. No usage marker: token reusable until expiry. Plain `===` compare opens timing oracle.

task: Detect credential-stuffing: many failed logins from one IP across many usernames. Implement velocity-based blocking using Redis.
reasoning: Per-user rate limit blocks one account's brute force. Per-IP across all usernames catches stuffing. Track distinct usernames per IP in a sliding window; over threshold = block. Cardinality estimation via HyperLogLog saves memory.
why_this_is_correct: PFADD/PFCOUNT use HyperLogLog — fixed 12KB memory regardless of cardinality, ~0.81% error margin. Catches the 'many usernames from one IP' pattern even at high volume. Block key independent of detection state so detection can keep accumulating.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Per-user rate limit only: stuffing attacks rotate usernames to stay below per-user thresholds. Tracking exact usernames in a Set: linear memory growth, attacker can fill memory with synthetic usernames.

task: List item must scroll into view when selected, but ref-callback fires too early (before parent layout). Use rAF inside the callback.
reasoning: Ref callback fires synchronously when DOM node mounts — before layout if parent is still computing. Schedule the scroll via requestAnimationFrame to ensure layout is settled. behavior='smooth' for accessibility.
why_this_is_correct: Ref callback only attached to the selected item — re-runs when selection changes (React detaches/re-attaches). rAF waits for layout before scrolling. block:'nearest' avoids unnecessary scroll if already visible.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Calling scrollIntoView directly in the ref callback: parent may not have laid out yet, scroll lands at wrong position. Using useEffect with selectedId: extra render, refs from prior render may be stale.

task: Return RFC 7807 Problem Details for errors so client libraries can consume them uniformly.
reasoning: RFC 7807 defines a JSON shape for HTTP error responses with type, title, status, detail, instance. Adoption beats ad-hoc { error: { ... } } because client SDKs auto-handle it. Subclass for typed problems.
why_this_is_correct: Standardized shape so clients can route by `type` URL. Extension fields (errors, retryAfter) preserved alongside the standard fields. Content-Type=application/problem+json signals to middleware/clients. instance is the request URI for correlation.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Custom error shape per endpoint: every client needs custom parsing. Forgetting Content-Type: clients treat as generic JSON, miss the discriminator. Putting extensions in detail string loses machine-readability.

task: Combine multiple business rules (active, premium, recently-active) into composable specifications, instead of one giant query builder.
reasoning: Specification pattern: each rule is an object with isSatisfiedBy(entity). Combine via and/or/not. Translate to query filters when persisting matters. Decouples 'what rule applies' from 'how to query for it'.
why_this_is_correct: Each spec is named, testable, composable. New rules add by writing one class. and/or/not enable readable composition that mirrors the business language. Pair with a separate translator if you also need DB-side filtering for the same rules.
what_breaks_naive_approach: One giant filter function with nested conditionals: hard to reuse subsets, hard to test, hard to combine. Boolean flags as args pile up into 7-parameter functions. Querying then filtering in memory wastes DB work.

task: Use a covered query: index includes all returned fields, so Mongo never reads the actual document. Show with explain.
reasoning: Index has a copy of the indexed values. If your query and projection only touch indexed fields, Mongo serves from the index without doc reads — much faster, often 10x for large docs.
why_this_is_correct: Compound index { status, lastSeenAt, email } contains everything the query returns. Projection { _id: 0, email: 1 } excludes _id (which isn't in this index). explain output shows totalDocsExamined=0 = covered. Subtle: must exclude _id with `_id: 0` because _id is implicitly included otherwise.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Default projection includes _id → query falls back to fetching docs. Index missing the email field → not covered, reads docs. Sorting by a field outside the index → in-memory sort, slower.

task: Updating an array sub-document loses concurrent edits. Use atomic $push/$pull/$set with positional operators.
reasoning: Reading the whole doc, modifying the array client-side, and saving = classic race. Mongo's $push, $pull, $set with `array.$.field` mutate atomically. Compound conditions with $elemMatch on filter side.
why_this_is_correct: $push atomically appends. $set with `comments.$.text` (positional operator) updates the matched array element. $pull removes elements matching the filter atomically. $elemMatch in the filter ensures the comment belongs to the author before allowing edit/delete.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Reading post, splicing comments, saving: two concurrent adds = one lost. Using arrayFilters incorrectly opens privilege escalation. Skipping authorId in filter lets anyone edit anyone's comment.

task: Function overloads vs generics: when each is the right choice, with a real example.
reasoning: Overloads describe a fixed set of signatures with distinct return types. Generics parameterize a single signature. Use overloads when the input shape determines the return shape categorically; generics when input flows through with the same shape.
why_this_is_correct: Overload signatures are independent — TS picks one based on the call. Generic preserves the parameter type through the function. pickKeys would need infinite overloads to handle every possible key combination; generic + Pick gives exact precision.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Generic for parse: T extends string|number|boolean returns T, but TS widens narrow callers (parse('a') returns string, not 'a'). Overload for identity: pages of overloads, still not exhaustive.

task: Send a private message between two users using Socket.IO rooms keyed by userId.
reasoning: Have each connecting user join a private room named after their userId. Sending a private message = emit to recipient's userId room. Multi-tab works naturally because both sockets join the same room.
why_this_is_correct: Per-user room is the canonical pattern. Sender also gets dm:sent so their UI updates immediately. ACK callback confirms send. Input validation rejects empty/oversized messages.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Tracking userId → socketId mapping manually breaks for multi-tab users and adds bookkeeping. Broadcasting to all sockets and filtering client-side leaks privacy.

task: Email verification flow: send a token-bearing link, verify on click, set emailVerified=true. Handle resends.
reasoning: Generate one-time token, store hash in user doc with expiry. Email link with raw token + userId. On click, compare and set verified. Resend invalidates previous tokens. Throttle resends.
why_this_is_correct: Hash stored — DB dump doesn't reveal active tokens. Generating a new token overwrites the old hash so previous links die (good for resend). Cooldown blocks spam. Argon2.verify is timing-safe.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Storing raw token: dump = mass account takeover. No cooldown: attacker spams reset emails as a UX-griefing attack. Not clearing token on success leaves it reusable.

task: Phone-number input with live masking that doesn't break cursor position on edits in the middle.
reasoning: Naive reformat on every change pushes the cursor to end. Track selection before/after change and restore mapped position. Strip non-digits, reformat, compute new cursor index in the masked string.
why_this_is_correct: Counts digits before cursor (stable across reformat), then maps that digit index back to a cursor in the new formatted string. rAF runs after React commits the new value to DOM. Pure formatter, no library needed.
what_breaks_naive_approach: setValue without cursor restoration: cursor snaps to end on every keystroke — typing middle is impossible. Setting cursor synchronously before React commits writes to a stale DOM value.

task: Order aggregate enforces invariants (e.g., total = sum of items) inside the domain object, not in services. Show the pattern.
reasoning: DDD aggregate: a cluster of objects guarded by a root that enforces invariants. Operations go through methods that validate state transitions. Service orchestrates; the aggregate owns the rules.
why_this_is_correct: Private fields + methods = invariants can't be bypassed. State transitions guarded (can't pay an unplaced order). totalCents is always derived — never stored in a stale field. rehydrate/toSnapshot is the persistence boundary.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Anemic object with public fields + service-side checks: same checks need to live everywhere mutations happen. Storing totalCents separately invites drift between items and total. Allowing partial mutations from any caller defeats encapsulation.

task: Infinite scroll without scroll-listener thrash: use IntersectionObserver on a sentinel element.
reasoning: Scroll listeners fire constantly and force expensive layout reads (offsetTop). IntersectionObserver tells you exactly when a sentinel enters the viewport. Place sentinel near bottom; load more when intersecting.
why_this_is_correct: IntersectionObserver runs off main-thread checks. rootMargin:500px starts loading before user reaches bottom — fetches arrive ahead of viewport. Guard against double-fire by checking loading flag. done short-circuits future fires.
what_breaks_naive_approach: window.scroll + getBoundingClientRect() recomputes layout on every scroll tick, kills FPS. No loading guard fires fetchPage 50 times during a fast scroll. Without rootMargin, user sees a 'loading' flash at the bottom every scroll.

task: Upsert race: two requests both try to create-or-update the same document by a unique key. One wins, the other crashes on duplicate key. Use findOneAndUpdate with upsert correctly.
reasoning: findOneAndUpdate(filter, update, { upsert: true }) is atomic — even under high concurrency, only one insert happens; the other becomes an update. Returning { new: true } gives the post-update doc. Handle the rare E11000 from concurrent unique-index races by retrying once.
why_this_is_correct: $setOnInsert applies only on insert (so createdAt etc. don't get overwritten on subsequent updates). One retry handles the documented race in MongoDB's upsert path where two concurrent upserts race the unique index. runValidators applies schema validation to the update.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `const existing = await find(); if (!existing) await create(); else await update();`: classic check-then-act race, both requests hit `!existing` and both call create — one crashes with E11000. No retry: even atomic upsert can race in rare cases per docs.

task: Custom type guard for runtime-validating an unknown payload against a known shape with discriminated branches.
reasoning: `is X` predicate narrows after a true return. Useful as a lightweight runtime check when you don't want Zod overhead. Build them carefully — false positives let bad data through; false negatives are merely annoying.
why_this_is_correct: Narrows by checking discriminator then each variant's payload. isArrayOf composes — `isArrayOf(data, isPet)` validates a list of pets. Number.isFinite rejects NaN/Infinity that typeof 'number' allows.
what_breaks_naive_approach: typeof value === 'object' alone returns true for null and arrays. Skipping per-variant payload checks lets `{ kind: 'cat' }` pass. Composing guards by hand for every array call site is tedious — isArrayOf factors it.

task: Configure Socket.IO CORS to allow a specific frontend origin with credentials, without exposing it to others.
reasoning: Socket.IO embeds its own CORS handling separate from Express middleware. Configure via cors option on the Server. credentials true requires explicit origin (no '*').
why_this_is_correct: Single allowed origin + credentials = browser sends cookies on handshake. Mismatched origin = CORS error before any handler runs. methods restricted to what Socket.IO actually uses.
what_breaks_naive_approach: origin '*' with credentials true is silently rejected by browsers. Skipping CORS entirely causes 'CORS error' for any non-same-origin client. Loose origins (regex `.*`) defeat the protection.

task: Compare an API key from a request header against a stored secret without leaking timing info.
reasoning: `===` exits at first different byte → response time correlates with shared prefix length → attacker reconstructs key one byte at a time. Use crypto.timingSafeEqual which runs in constant time regardless of input differences.
why_this_is_correct: Length check returns 401 for wrong length (timingSafeEqual throws on unequal lengths). For equal-length compares, timingSafeEqual runs in constant time. Same error message for length mismatch and value mismatch — no enum oracle.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `provided === expectedKey`: timing leak. Trim/case normalization on the key changes its byte length, breaking timing-safe compare. Returning different errors for 'wrong length' vs 'wrong value' enables enumeration.

task: Component does expensive work in useState initializer on every render. Use lazy initializer to compute once.
reasoning: useState(expensiveCompute()) calls expensiveCompute on every render even though only the first call's result is used. Pass a function: useState(() => expensiveCompute()) — called once.
why_this_is_correct: Function form is invoked only on mount. parseHugeJson runs once even though the component re-renders many times. Same pattern works for useReducer's third lazy initializer arg.
what_breaks_naive_approach: useState(parseHugeJson()): parses JSON on every render — wasteful and may show stale data if storage changed mid-component-life. Performance hit grows with how heavy the compute is.

task: Async error in onClick handler isn't caught by Error Boundary. Show why and the correct pattern.
reasoning: Error Boundaries catch errors during RENDER, not during event handlers or async code. For event-handler errors, use try/catch and route to a centralized error reporter + maybe a notification.
why_this_is_correct: Wraps every async call in try/catch + state so UI can display + telemetry can record. Errors don't propagate to Error Boundary, but the hook surfaces them locally. Returning T | null forces caller to handle the failure path.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Throwing inside onClick: React swallows it, Error Boundary doesn't catch, user sees nothing. setError inside an unawaited promise sometimes sets state after unmount.

task: Add caching to a UserService without modifying it — use the Decorator pattern.
reasoning: Open/Closed: extend behavior without modifying source. Decorator wraps the original and adds responsibilities. Same interface, drop-in replacement.
why_this_is_correct: Decorators stack: log → cache → db. Each decorator obeys the same interface so consumers don't care which layer they touch. Adding a new cross-cutting concern (metrics, auth) means another decorator class, not edits to existing services.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Adding cache logic inside DbUserReadService violates SRP and forces conditional caching by env. Inheritance (subclass DbUserReadService) couples implementations; only single-extend.

task: Enable HTTP response compression in Express with sensible thresholds and exclusions.
reasoning: compression() middleware gzips responses above a threshold. Skip for already-compressed content types (images, videos). Skip for SSE streams (chunked compression interferes). Brotli is better than gzip for static assets but compression middleware uses gzip.
why_this_is_correct: 1KB threshold avoids compressing tiny responses where overhead exceeds savings. Level 6 is the sweet spot between CPU and compression ratio. Excluding images/videos (already compressed) and SSE (chunked stream stays open) prevents bugs.
what_breaks_naive_approach: compression() with defaults compresses 200-byte JSON responses, wasting CPU. Compressing SSE breaks event-by-event flushing (client doesn't receive until enough data accumulates). High compression level (9) dominates CPU under load.

task: Cross-process named lock using PostgreSQL advisory locks for periodic jobs that must run on exactly one instance.
reasoning: Multiple app instances may try to run the same cron job. Advisory locks (pg_try_advisory_lock) give app-level mutual exclusion without table writes. Release explicitly or rely on session-bound auto-release.
why_this_is_correct: pg_try_advisory_lock is non-blocking — returns false if another session holds it. Tied to the session/connection, so a crashed instance's lock auto-releases when the connection closes. Hashing the name to a bigint key avoids collisions and lets you use string names ergonomically.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Row-based lock table: ABA problem if a process crashes mid-update — lock never released. SELECT FOR UPDATE on a 'jobs' row works but holds a transaction open. In-memory mutex: only protects one instance.

task: Compose utility types to derive PatchInput (writable subset), CreateInput (required-on-create fields), and ViewModel from one source type.
reasoning: Single source of truth for the entity; derive related shapes via mapped types. Avoid hand-maintaining N similar interfaces. TS has Partial, Required, Pick, Omit — combine them.
why_this_is_correct: WritableUserFields stays in sync with UserEntity — adding a field auto-includes it unless excluded explicitly. Patch type is always partial. ViewModel strips sensitive fields. password vs passwordHash makes the input/output distinction explicit.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hand-maintaining UserCreateDto/UserUpdateDto/UserResponse: drifts on field changes, requires multiple updates per refactor. Sending the entity through API leaks passwordHash to clients.

task: Export Socket.IO metrics (connections, room counts, event rates) as Prometheus gauges and counters.
reasoning: Prom-client provides counter/gauge/histogram. Update gauges on a schedule (connection count) and counters on events. Set up /metrics endpoint scraped by Prometheus.
why_this_is_correct: Default metrics (CPU, memory) give Node-level visibility for free. onAny tracks every event without per-event instrumentation. Label cardinality kept low (event names truncated, no userId labels) — high-cardinality labels destroy Prometheus.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Adding userId or roomId labels to counters: cardinality explodes, Prometheus becomes unusable. No event-name truncation lets attackers send unique events to inflate label space. Tracking metrics in plain Map loses the established Prom semantics scrapers expect.

task: Render user-submitted Markdown safely in React without dangerouslySetInnerHTML escaping legitimate tags.
reasoning: Marked → HTML → DOMPurify → render. The allowlist approach (default-deny) blocks unknown tags. Configure to keep common formatting but kill scripts, iframes, event handlers.
why_this_is_correct: Allowlist of tags + attributes + URI scheme = anything new is blocked. SVG/math forbidden because they're vectors for embedded scripts. rel=noopener on external links prevents tab-nabbing.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Skipping sanitization: every in user content runs script. Blocklist approach (forbid script only) misses new vectors. Permissive URI regexp allows javascript: URLs in href.

task: Wrap useReducer with a middleware system (logger, persistence) without external library.
reasoning: Redux's middleware is just `next => action => result`. Same pattern works for useReducer. Wrap dispatch with middleware functions that can intercept actions before they hit the reducer.
why_this_is_correct: Chain composition mirrors Redux exactly. reduceRight ensures the first middleware in the array runs first. getState always returns the freshest state via ref. Persist middleware runs after the reducer (so it sees the new state).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hand-wiring logging inside the reducer breaks purity. localStorage in the reducer is a side effect that fails under StrictMode double-invocation. Building a global middleware chain outside React loses per-component scoping.

task: Serve a React SPA from Express with API routes that take precedence over the catch-all index.html.
reasoning: SPA uses client-side routing — server must serve index.html for any path the SPA owns. But /api/* must 404 with JSON, not HTML. Order matters: API routes first, static files, then catch-all SPA route.
why_this_is_correct: API routes first → API takes precedence. Catch-all for /api/* returns JSON 404 via the error pipeline. Static files have long cache (immutable hashes). index.html no-cache so deploys are picked up. Final * route serves index.html for SPA navigation.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Catch-all before API: every API call returns index.html, browsers parse HTML as JSON and explode. Caching index.html with long TTL: users stuck on old SPA bundle after deploy. Serving static with index:true conflicts with the catch-all.

task: Replace direct `new Date()` in services via injected Clock factory; show how this enables time-travel in tests.
reasoning: Tests for date-sensitive logic (expiry, schedules) need controlled time. Inject a Clock. Real impl uses Date.now; test impl is a manual clock you advance.
why_this_is_correct: ManualClock lets tests advance time deterministically — no `sleep`, no jest fake timers globals. SystemClock for production. Same interface so production code is unaware.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `Date.now()` in code: tests must mock globals (brittle, leaks between tests). Adding `now: number` parameter to every method pollutes APIs and forgetting one creates inconsistent behavior.

task: Defer client hydration of non-critical components below the fold using React 19 Suspense + dynamic import.
reasoning: Streaming SSR with selective hydration: critical UI hydrates first; below-the-fold defers. lazy + Suspense at the route level achieves this without external frameworks.
why_this_is_correct: Each lazy boundary is its own chunk; React 19 streams server-rendered placeholder, hydrates the wrapper above first, then independently hydrates each Suspense block. User can interact with the article before comments/recs load.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Static import of all components: everything hydrates in one go, blocking interaction. No Suspense boundary: a slow component blocks the whole tree.

task: BullMQ job that calls a flaky third-party API with exponential backoff retry and dead-letter queue after N attempts.
reasoning: BullMQ retries failed jobs with configurable backoff. Distinguish retryable errors (network, 5xx) from permanent (4xx, bad input) — don't waste retries on the latter. Failed jobs after exhaust go to a DLQ for inspection.
why_this_is_correct: Exponential backoff (2s, 4s, 8s, 16s, 32s) gives transient failures time to clear. PermanentError short-circuits retry — invalid email won't fix itself. DLQ captures both permanent and exhausted jobs with context (lastError) for ops triage.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Retrying validation errors burns 5 attempts on a permanently bad input — wastes time and may charge for API calls. No DLQ: failed jobs vanish, ops never know what was lost. Linear retry: thundering herd when upstream recovers.

task: Build a typed get function that walks a nested object path string ('user.profile.name') and returns the right value type.
reasoning: Template literal types recurse on dotted paths. PathOf<T> generates all dot-separated key paths; ValueAtPath<T,P> resolves the value type. Powers libraries like react-hook-form's path-based field access.
why_this_is_correct: Recursive type captures arbitrarily nested paths. getPath signature forces P to be a valid path of T at compile time — typos like 'profile.nme' fail compilation. ValueAtPath narrows return to the exact type at that path.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `get(obj, 'a.b.c')` returning unknown forces casts at every call site. Hand-typed overloads can't enumerate every path of a deep object. Runtime-only path access loses TS safety entirely.

task: Log every event a socket receives during development without modifying handlers.
reasoning: socket.onAny(handler) fires for every event. Useful in dev/debug; disable in prod or guard by env. Pair with onAnyOutgoing for emit-side logging.
why_this_is_correct: onAny / onAnyOutgoing are the canonical interception hooks. Argument truncation prevents log explosion. Production guard avoids leaking payloads in prod logs.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Wrapping every handler manually = work and brittleness. Logging without truncation can dump 100MB payloads. Leaving dev logger on in prod = PII in logs + perf hit.

task: Roll out a strict CSP gradually: report-only first, fix violations from telemetry, then enforce.
reasoning: Strict CSP often breaks unknown legacy code on rollout. Report-Only mode logs violations without blocking; analyze reports, fix sources, then flip to enforcement. Trusted Types (where supported) hardens DOM sinks against XSS.
why_this_is_correct: Report-Only header lets browsers report violations without blocking — safe rollout. require-trusted-types-for adds defense-in-depth for browsers that support it. Per-request nonce avoids unsafe-inline. report-uri (or newer report-to) feeds violations back for monitoring.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Flipping straight to enforced strict CSP breaks production pages with unknown inline scripts. Forgetting report-uri means you have no way to find what needs fixing. Reusing nonces across requests defeats the protection.

task: useAsyncCallback hook: wrap an async function in a loading flag + cancel previous calls on rerun.
reasoning: Common UI pattern: button kicks off async work, disable while loading, ignore late responses if user retries quickly. Encapsulate in a hook.
why_this_is_correct: Generation counter discards stale resolutions. loading flag wraps the whole call. Errors land in error state for UI consumption. Returning null on stale lets callers ignore late returns deterministically.
what_breaks_naive_approach: setState after unmount warning if user navigates away mid-call. Late successful call from a stale invocation overwrites a newer failed call's state. Without try/finally, throw paths leave loading=true.

task: Add a per-route timeout that aborts handlers exceeding N ms and returns 504 to the client.
reasoning: Handlers that hang block worker capacity. Per-route timeout: race the handler against a setTimeout that aborts via AbortController and sends 504. Handler must respect signal for true cancellation.
why_this_is_correct: req.abortSignal exposed for handler to pass to fetch/DB calls that support cancellation. headersSent guard avoids 'cannot set headers after sent' error if handler responds first. clearTimeout on finish/close prevents the timer firing post-response.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Setting Express timeout via req.setTimeout doesn't cancel the running handler — it just fires a 'timeout' event that you must handle. Without AbortSignal, slow DB query continues consuming connections even after the response is sent.

task: Ports-and-adapters: separate the domain from infrastructure so the domain has no Mongoose/Express imports.
reasoning: Hexagonal architecture: domain depends on interfaces (ports); infrastructure implements them (adapters). The domain becomes pure and reusable. Wiring happens at the composition root.
why_this_is_correct: Domain only knows interface; can be unit-tested with in-memory fake. Swap to Postgres = write PostgresUserRepo, no changes in domain. Composition root is the only place that wires concrete impls — clear seam.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Importing Mongoose in domain services: now domain depends on the database. Hard to test without spinning up Mongo. Migrating DBs touches dozens of files.

task: Read-only endpoints that don't need Mongoose Documents should use .lean() to skip hydration overhead.
reasoning: Hydration creates Mongoose Documents with getters, methods, change tracking — meaningful CPU per doc. For read paths, plain objects via lean() are 5-10x faster.
why_this_is_correct: lean() = no Mongoose ceremony, returns POJO matching the typed shape. Projection limits the returned fields. Generic on lean lets you assert the shape without `as` casts.
what_breaks_naive_approach: findById without lean returns a Document — slower to construct and JSON-serialize. If you assign to `.name = ...` and forget to call save(), nothing persists. Lean docs lack model methods.

task: Expose a /health endpoint that returns Socket.IO connection count and Redis pub/sub status for k8s probes.
reasoning: Liveness probe should be cheap and rarely fail (only restart on real wedge). Readiness probe checks dependencies. Separate them: liveness = process alive; readiness = ready to serve.
why_this_is_correct: Liveness just returns 200 — keeps pod alive if process loop is healthy. Readiness pings dependencies; failure removes pod from LB rotation. Latency in checks lets dashboards spot creeping problems before outright failure.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Same endpoint for both: a transient Redis hiccup restarts your pods. Heavy checks in liveness (e.g., DB query): probe times out under load, pod restart cascade. No latency exposure = harder to debug slow dependencies.

task: Encrypt sensitive fields (SSN, bank routing number) at the application layer before storing in Mongo.
reasoning: DB-level encryption protects against disk theft; app-level field encryption protects against DB compromise + insider threats. AES-GCM provides authenticated encryption. Use a per-record IV.
why_this_is_correct: AES-256-GCM provides confidentiality + integrity. 12-byte IV per encryption ensures uniqueness (96 bits is GCM's standard). Auth tag prevents tampering — wrong key/ciphertext fails decryption noisily. Version field allows future algorithm rotation.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Reusing the same IV: catastrophic for GCM (key recovery possible). AES-CBC without HMAC allows tampering. Hardcoding the key in source: rotation impossible, leak = total compromise.

task: Find optional keys of a type with a mapped-types trick. Useful for forcing defaults at the boundary.
reasoning: There's no built-in OptionalKeys<T> but you can derive it with the {} extends Pick trick. Powers utilities that require defaults for every optional field.
why_this_is_correct: `{} extends Pick<T,K>` is true iff K is optional (Pick of an optional key allows an empty object). Mapped type collects matching keys. Type-level enforcement means adding a new optional field to Config forces you to add a default — compile error otherwise.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hand-maintained list of optional keys drifts. Defaults provided as a fresh object don't get type-checked against the actual optionality of T.

task: Throttle a specific event type per user (typing-indicator capped at 5/sec) without affecting other events.
reasoning: Per-event token bucket per user. Cheap in-memory with Map; for cluster use Redis. Refill rate matters more than exact bucket size — typing indicator at >5Hz adds nothing.
why_this_is_correct: Token bucket gives bursty traffic some headroom (capacity) but enforces long-run rate (refill). Pure function on the bucket — easy to test. Silently drops over-limit events for typing (better UX than erroring).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Global rate limit per user: typing burst eats budget for actual messages. Fixed-window counter has boundary spikes. Without throttle, mobile keyboard repeats can send hundreds of events per second.

task: Selector returns a new array reference every call, forcing re-renders. Add shallow equality to skip when contents are the same.
reasoning: Object/array selectors with Object.is equality always re-render because [].includes() returns a new array. Shallow equality compares array contents element-wise (or top-level object keys) — same contents skip the re-render.
why_this_is_correct: Two-step: Object.is fast path, then element/key shallow compare. Doesn't recurse — by design, deep comparisons are usually a sign of wrong store shape. Plays well with state-101 selector hook.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Default Object.is comparison: any selector returning `items.filter(...)` rerenders every store change. Deep equality is correct but expensive; shallow is the sweet spot for normalized stores.

task: Return JSON 404 for any unknown route instead of Express's default HTML page.
reasoning: Easy fix: register a catch-all middleware after all routes that responds with JSON. Pair with a centralized error pipeline for consistency.
why_this_is_correct: Catch-all middleware after routes covers any unmatched method+path. Same shape as other error responses for client convenience.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Default Express 404 returns 'Cannot GET /foo' HTML — confuses JSON-consuming clients. Skipping the catch-all means missing routes hang or return ambiguous defaults.

task: A barrel index.ts that re-exports everything from a feature module is fine, but make it ENFORCE the public API by hiding internals.
reasoning: Barrel index.ts at the feature root re-exports public symbols. Use ESLint rule + folder convention: internal/ folder is unimported from outside. Co-located tests stay accessible to the feature.
why_this_is_correct: Barrel exposes only what's intended public. ESLint rule prevents accidental cross-feature imports of `internal/`. Tests in the same feature folder can import internals; consumers across features cannot.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Barrel that exports `*` from every file leaks internals — consumers couple to repo classes. Without an ESLint rule, the convention drifts. Single `src/utils` folder with no module boundaries becomes a tangle.

task: useDeferredValue lets you render a stale version of a value during heavy work, keeping inputs responsive. Demonstrate vs useTransition.
reasoning: useDeferredValue defers a value's update so React renders the previous value first. Best when the source updates rapidly (search input) and you want the consumer to lag. useTransition wraps state updates, marking them as non-urgent. They overlap; useDeferredValue is simpler for derived rendering.
why_this_is_correct: Input updates instantly (urgent). Deferred value updates after React has free time. isStale flag fades the list to hint to user. Simpler than useTransition for value-driven rendering.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Direct setState fires synchronous filter on every keystroke — visible input lag with 10k items. useTransition would work but requires wrapping the setter, not the consumer; useDeferredValue is closer to the actual problem.

task: Promise.all vs Promise.allSettled: choosing the right one for fan-out where partial failure is acceptable.
reasoning: Promise.all rejects fast — first failure aborts the whole batch with that error. Promise.allSettled waits for all to complete and returns a per-promise status. Use all when one failure invalidates the result; allSettled for independent operations.
why_this_is_correct: Notifications: one channel failing shouldn't block others — allSettled. Validation: any one invalid means we reject the whole batch — all. The distinction maps to the business semantics, not personal preference.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Using all for notifications: SMS fails, email succeeds, you return error and never know two of three got through. Using allSettled for validation: easy to forget to inspect statuses, accept invalid input silently.

task: Use a const object + union type instead of enum for better tree-shaking and string-literal narrowing.
reasoning: TS enums emit runtime code, don't tree-shake cleanly, and produce reverse mappings (number-valued ones). const objects + derived unions give the same ergonomics with zero runtime overhead.
why_this_is_correct: const + as const gives you the namespace ergonomics of enum AND lets you pass raw string literals where the type expects them. No runtime enum object, no reverse mapping. Same API for consumers, smaller bundle.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `enum` with default settings ships runtime objects, prevents proper tree-shaking. Mixed string/number enums confuse callers. const enums require special compiler flags and break across module boundaries.

task: Add hCaptcha/Turnstile verification to sensitive endpoints (signup, password reset) to deter bots.
reasoning: CAPTCHA challenges happen client-side; server verifies the resulting token by calling the provider's verify endpoint. Mark high-risk endpoints to require the token; don't require for low-risk ones (UX cost).
why_this_is_correct: Single-purpose middleware, attach only to sensitive endpoints. Provider's IP arg helps detect fraud (mismatched IP between challenge and submission). Verify against provider's endpoint server-side — never trust client-only verification.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Trusting client-side captcha widget without server verify: trivially bypassed. Adding captcha to every endpoint = UX death. Skipping IP arg loses one of the provider's risk signals.
